result:
The first thing that happens when I open the blank page is the sound of the refrigerator in the next room coughing itself awake. It is 3:12 a.m. Eastern, the hour when the house decides it is finally honest with itself: floorboards sigh, the hot-water pipe ticks like a metronome for ghosts, and the ceiling fan repeats the same four-note lullaby it has hummed since 1997. I used to think these noises were interruptions. Now I suspect they are the first sentences of whatever I am about to write, spoken in a dialect older than language.

A thousand words is roughly four pages double-spaced, or one shy confession, or the distance between a lightning bug and lightning, according to the old joke that never gets a laugh. I have been given the length of a shepherd’s sling and told to topple something vast. The deal is I can’t prepare, can’t google “what should I write,” can’t even pretend I have a plan. I am the kid standing at the edge of the pool whose friends count down from five and always skip three. Momentum is cruel, but it is also merciful; it doesn’t leave room for the fear to put on shoes.

So here is the thing that has been following me like a second shadow: I don’t know how to finish anything. I start pots of coffee, friendships, journals, pregnancies of thought, and then leave them half-done on the counter like avocados that will never ripen now. My hard drive is a foster home for abandoned first chapters: a novel about a cartographer who erases countries faster than nationalism can invent them; a short story where rain is a federally regulated substance; a manifesto arguing children should be required to teach adults how to play again. The folders are labeled things like “soon” and “after” and “when I am brave.” They sit there humming, radioactive with possibility, and I keep adding more coffee grounds to the compost of almost.

Last week I found a photograph of my mother at twenty-two, standing on the roof of a Manila apartment building in 1979, hair longer than the decade, holding a beer she never drank because the bottle was a borrowed prop. She is squinting into a future that includes me but doesn’t know it yet. On the back she wrote, “I wish I could remember what I was waiting for.” The sentence ends without punctuation, as if the thought simply stepped off the edge of the paper and kept falling. I keep the picture tucked inside whatever notebook I am pretending to fill, a passport from a country called Before. Some days I stare at it the way a lost driver stares at a map that refuses to confess where the detour began.

The experts say the way to finish is to lower your standards until they are underground, then dig. So here I am, tunneling beneath my own expectations, wearing the headlamp of a deadline. I keep hitting pipes, roots, the fossilized bones of other drafts. Somewhere down here is the first poem I ever memorized—Frost’s “Nothing Gold Can Stay”—and the accompanying memory of standing in front of a classroom of eighth graders who smelled like cafeteria pizza and adolescent panic, chanting four-line chunks of eternity while Mrs. Cortez banged a yardstick against the doorframe for emphasis. She made us say it four times, faster each round, until the words compressed into pure percussion: Nature’s first green is gold, / Her hardest hue to hold. I didn’t know then that poetry could be a drum, that meaning could be the echo after the drum, that I would spend the rest of my life trying to hold the gold between my teeth long enough to taste it.

I read once that the human heart circulates blood through the body in one minute flat, which means every sixty seconds you get a brand-new river riding the same old canoe. I think about that when I feel stale, when the day tastes like reheated coffee and I can’t remember why I walked into the kitchen. The body is a loyal river, forever trying to surprise itself. It doesn’t know how to quit, even when the mind writes resignation letters in the dark. I try to borrow that stubbornness, cup my hands around it the way you might carry a firefly without crushing its light. The trick is not to trap it, only to guide it toward the next sentence before it blinks out.

Outside, the neighbor’s security light flicks on because a moth has ambitious dreams. The moth is a small moon, pinging off the fixture like a drunk spark. I want to tell it the story of Icarus, but Icarus never listened either. The difference is that when you fall from a 40-watt bulb, the ocean is only a porch step away and no one writes songs about your hubris. Still, the moth keeps flapping, translating its soft body into a yes against the glass, and I keep writing, because saying yes to something impossible is the only way I know to stay alive.

When I was nine I believed every word I wrote was being archived by angels who needed evidence for a cosmic trial in which I was both defendant and star witness. I wrote with a seriousness that alarmed my parents: lists of birds ranked by moral character, letters of apology to the soap I had wasted, a tiny play starring my left and right shoes who argued over whether walking was a form of colonialism. I sealed everything in envelopes and buried them behind the garage, a time capsule for the Day of Reckoning. Sometimes I dream the angels are still digging, their wings covered in loam, patiently waiting for me to conclude the final sentence so they can close the file labeled “Reasons to Let Her Stay.”

I am down to 237 words now, the last miles of a road that began as wilderness. The refrigerator has stopped its monologue; either it forgave me or ran out of things to accuse. The moth has moved on to brighter failures. In the silence I can almost hear the heartbeat of the house, that steady 1-minute loop remaking itself. I think of my mother on the roof, of the gold that refuses to be held, of the unfinished pots and novels and the mercy of momentum. I think of the angels, how they must be tired, how they must want to go home. I close my eyes and type the last thing, not because it is perfect, but because it is next:

Begin again.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
